content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


June 11, 1992


Dr. Sylvia Alatorre Alva
7615 Pacific Blvd.
Huntington Park, CA  90255

Re:  Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-92-323

Dear Dr. Alva:

     This is in response to your request for confirmation of
telephone advice regarding the campaign disclosure provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1  You are requesting this
advice on behalf of Salvador Alva.

     Your letter, dated May 26, 1992, accurately summarizes the
telephone advice it provided to you that the Political Reform Act
does not prohibit a judicial candidate from identifying his or her
opponent's political party in mass mailings. The Commission only
has jurisdiction over the Political Reform Act. You may wish to
contact the Attorney General's office or the Secretary of State to
determine whether any other law may apply.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5662.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By:  Sandra L. Taylor
     Political Reform Consultant

1  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916)322-5660

sections.question_synthetic: Whether the Political Reform Act prohibits a judicial candidate from identifying his or her opponent's political party in mass mailings.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit a judicial candidate from identifying his or her opponent's political party in mass mailings. The FPPC only has jurisdiction over the Political Reform Act and recommends contacting the Attorney General's office or Secretary of State to determine if other laws may apply.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a confirmation letter of previously provided telephone advice. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections exist, but the advice is clearly stated in the body of the letter. Document references Political Reform Act (Gov. Code §§81000-91015) and California Code of Regulations Title 2, Division 6.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the Political Reform Act prohibits a judicial candidate from identifying his or her opponent's political party in mass mailings.

CONCLUSION: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit a judicial candidate from identifying his or her opponent's political party in mass mailings. The FPPC only has jurisdiction over the Political Reform Act and recommends contacting the Attorney General's office or Secretary of State to determine if other laws may apply.

Your letter, dated May 26, 1992, accurately summarizes the telephone advice it provided to you that the Political Reform Act does not prohibit a judicial candidate from identifying his or her opponent's political party in mass mailings. The Commission only has jurisdiction over the Political Reform Act. You may wish to contact the Attorney General's office or the Secretary of State to determine whether any other law may apply. If you have any further questions regarding this matter, please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5662. Sincerely, Scott Hallabrin Acting General Counsel By: Sandra L. embedding.summary: FPPC confirms telephone advice to Dr. Alva regarding Salvador Alva's campaign that the Political Reform Act does not prohibit judicial candidates from identifying opponents' political party affiliation in mass mailings.
